original_reasoning: Greek denotes a kind of shoe or sock, while Arabic includes a kind of cloth; both sit in the concrete textile-wear domain.
Arabic imprisonment is binding or enclosure, and kinship preserves the idea of being bound together.
A stringed instrument depends on stretched tied strings, giving a plausible concrete link.
Arabic mating involves pressing and working the body; Greek is 'soften, knead with the hands'. Shared concrete bodily manipulation gives a plausible chain.
A food pen or built enclosure conceptually matches containment and fullness.
Arabic loud cry and Greek calling both center on emitting a strong voice.

Arabic bast (b-s-t) = a kind of walking/travel (al-bast min al-sayr), and also Bust city in Khurasan; Greek apebeseto = he stepped off / went away (aorist of apobaino, 'to step away'). Genuine overlap on the concept of walking/stepping/going away.

البثنة=الرملة الليّنة (soft sandy ground). πολυβενθής=deep-bottomed, of great depth. Soft sand suggests deep terrain (you sink into it), and πολυβενθής describes deep ground/seabed. Both describe ground with depth quality. The terrain-depth concept is shared.
